does: This paper systematically evaluates theory-of-mind performance across GPT-4 and multiple GPT-3.5 variants, measuring their ability to reason about agents' beliefs, goals, and mental states. The authors benchmark these models on a theory-of-mind task set, comparing zero-shot performance against in-context learning approaches using two-shot chain-of-thought reasoning and step-by-step thinking instructions. Their key finding is that RLHF-trained models show substantial improvement through prompting, with GPT-4 reaching nearly 80% accuracy in zero-shot settings and achieving 100% accuracy when supplied with in-context learning prompts, though still falling short of human performance (87%) in zero-shot evaluation before prompting optimization.
connects: This work builds on established research in theory-of-mind evaluation and in-context learning for LLMs. Theory-of-mind benchmarking has been a central concern in evaluating machine reasoning capabilities, extending classical cognitive science tasks to modern language models. The paper connects to broader investigations of how prompting techniques like chain-of-thought reasoning can unlock latent reasoning abilities in LLMs, a line of work that emerged from prior studies on prompt engineering and its effectiveness across reasoning tasks. It also engages with research on the differences between base models and RLHF-trained variants, building on findings that human feedback training systematically improves performance on complex reasoning benchmarks.
enables: This research points toward developing more sophisticated prompting strategies for eliciting nuanced social reasoning from LLMs, with implications for human-AI interaction and collaboration tasks requiring mentalizing. The substantial performance gaps between zero-shot and prompted settings suggest opportunities for designing better few-shot exemplars and reasoning scaffolds tailored to theory-of-mind reasoning. Future work could explore whether these improvements transfer to more naturalistic social reasoning scenarios, investigate failure modes of current prompting approaches, and examine how to narrow the remaining gap between prompted LLM performance and human accuracy for genuine advances in social understanding capabilities.
